papers.title: A Massive Young Runaway Star in W49 North
papers.author_names: Luis F. Rodríguez Roberto Galván-Madrid Joel Sanchez-Bermudez Christopher G. De Pree

abstract.text: We analyzed high angular resolution 45.5 GHz images of the W49 North massive star forming region obtained in 1998 and 2016 with the Very Large Array. Most of the ultracompact HII regions show no detectable changes over the time interval of the observations. However, subcomponents B1, B2, G2a and G2c have increased its peak flux densities by values in the range of 3.8 to 21.4 \\\\%. Most interestingly, the cometary region C clearly shows proper motions that at the distance of the region are equivalent to a velocity of 76$\\\\pm$6 km s$^{-1}$ in the plane of the sky. We interpret this region as the ionized bowshock produced by a runaway O6 ZAMS star that was ejected from the eastern edge of Welch's ring about 6,400 years ago.

brief.tldr: [observational astrophysics letter] High-angular-resolution radio observations of W49 North reveal a cometary bowshock region with measurable proper motion (~76 km/s in the plane of the sky), interpreted as ionized gas swept by a massive O6 main-sequence runaway star ejected from Welch’s ring ~6,400 years ago. Concurrently, flux increases of 3.8–21.4% are detected in subcomponents B1, B2, G2a, and G2c, suggesting dynamical interactions or variability in the ultracompact HII regions, with no significant changes in most of the region's structure.

source_fields.title: A Massive Young Runaway Star in W49 North

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study identifies source C in the Welch ring of W49N as a young, massive runaway O6-type star ejected approximately 6400 years ago, evidenced by large proper motions and a blueshifted line-of-sight velocity, while also reporting flux increases in other components consistent with dynamic massive star formation. Introduction: This paper identifies a massive runaway star ejected from the Welch ring of Ultracompact HII regions in W49N, providing new evidence for dynamical ejections in a massive star-forming region and addressing the problem of understanding the timing and mechanisms of runaway star formation in dense, young clusters. Image Analysis: The observed offset between the 1998 and 2016 images is primarily due to instrumental and observational effects rather than proper motion, with alignment and flux calibration achieved through systematic adjustments that reveal no significant changes in the positions or emission of bright components like A1 and A2. Multiepoch Data: This section presents multiepoch Q-band VLA observations from 1998 and 2016, calibrated and aligned to enable a high-resolution comparison of the compact radio source structure over a 18-year baseline. Flux Variability: Flux variability in components B and G, with increases at 7 mm and decreases at 3.6 cm, is consistent with the formation of an optically thin, compact HII region due to sudden molecular gas ionization, aligning with theoretical predictions of time variability in UC/HC HII regions. Radio Source C as a Runaway from W49N: Radio source C exhibits significant proper motion consistent with being a runaway star ejected from the eastern edge of Welch’s ring approximately 6,400 years ago, with its emission likely arising from a bow shock due to its motion through the interstellar medium.

abstract: We interpret this region as the ionized bowshock produced by a runaway O6 ZAMS star that was ejected from the eastern edge of Welch's ring about 6,400 years ago.
title: A Massive Young Runaway Star in W49 North

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents proper motion measurements from VLA radio observations of a specific runaway star candidate (cometary region C in W49 North) with velocity calculation and interpretation as ejected O6 star.

papers.title: The Hills Mechanism and the Galactic Center S-stars

abstract.text: Our Galactic center contains young stars, including the few million year old clockwise disk between 0.05 and 0.5 pc from the Galactic center, and the S-star cluster of B-type stars at a galactocentric distance of ~0.01 pc. Recent observations suggest the S-stars are remnants of tidally disrupted binaries from the clockwise disk. In particular, Koposov et al. 2020 discovered a hypervelocity star that was ejected from the Galactic center 5 Myr ago, with a velocity vector consistent with the disk. We perform a detailed study of this binary disruption scenario. First, we quantify the plausible range of binary semimajor axes in the disk. Dynamical evaporation of such binaries is dominated by other disk stars rather than by the isotropic stellar population. For the expected range of semimajor axes in the disk, binary tidal disruptions can reproduce the observed S-star semimajor axis distribution. Reproducing the observed thermal eccentricity distribution of the S-stars requires an additional relaxation process. The flight time of the Koposov star suggests that this process must be effective within 10 Myr. We consider three possibilities: (i) scalar resonant relaxation from the observed isotropic star cluster, (ii) torques from the clockwise disk, and (iii) an intermediate-mass black hole. We conclude that the first and third mechanisms are fast enough to reproduce the observed S-star eccentricity distribution. Finally, we show that the primary star from an unequal-mass binary would be deposited at larger semimajor axes than the secondary, possibly explaining the dearth of O stars among the S-stars.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: Hills mechanism and tidal binary disruption LLM confirmed: Directly discusses hypervelocity star ejection via Hills mechanism from Galactic center, mentions specific hypervelocity star discovery and binary disruption scenario for high-velocity stars

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The Hills mechanism produces bound stellar remnants on highly eccentric orbits ($e > 0.96$) with semimajor axes between $\\sim 10^{-3}$ and $1$ pc, and for thermal eccentric binaries, the resulting semimajor axis distribution inside 0.05 pc is statistically consistent with the S-star distribution (KS test probability 0.21), though the parabolic orbit approximation breaks down for semimajor axes beyond this radius. Conclusion: The conclusion suggests that tidal disruption of binary stars in the clockwise disk can explain the S-star population's semimajor axis distribution, though reproducing their observed eccentricity distribution requires either scalar resonant relaxation or a faint IMBH, with the latter being marginally consistent with current constraints. Discussion: In simulated binary–SMBH encounters, approximately 20% result in stellar collisions (vs. 50% in disruptions) under circular and thermal eccentricity distributions, with collision rates increasing only slightly in more realistic eccentric orbits, and hypervelocity stars ejected at velocities up to the 92nd percentile—consistent with observed detections—when accounting for realistic binary orbital dynamics. Introduction: This paper proposes a refined Hills mechanism model in which binaries from the young clockwise disk are tidally disrupted near the supermassive black hole, naturally explaining the S-stars' observed age, velocity distribution, and thermal eccentricity through subsequent stellar relaxation, offering a novel and parsimonious solution to the long-standing problem of the S-stars' origin. Binary disruption ensemble: This section describes a Monte Carlo simulation ensemble of stellar binary encounters with a supermassive black hole, where binary properties—semimajor axis, mass, and eccentricity—are drawn from physically motivated distributions constrained by tidal effects, gravitational wave emission, and dynamical evaporation. Eccentric disk instability: Eccentric disk instability in the Galactic center can lead to tidal disruption of stars and binaries through precession and torque effects, though observed eccentricity distributions remain debated due to potential contamination from binaries and non-disk stars. Appendix A Hills Approximation: The Hills approximation accurately models binary star separations and positions after disruption, with the iterated version agreeing within 1% of high-precision three-body integration results, especially at late times. Galactic center Binary Population: This section analyzes the dynamical constraints on binary star properties in the Galactic center's clockwise disk, determining that gravitational wave inspiral and Roche lobe overflow dominate over evaporation and Kozai–Lidov effects for young binaries. Reproducing the observed eccentricity distribution of the S-stars: This section demonstrates that scalar resonant relaxation, potentially enhanced by massive perturbers like stellar mass black holes, can reproduce the observed eccentricity distribution of the S-stars within the required timescale of ~10⁷ years.

title: The Hills Mechanism and the Galactic Center S-stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical/dynamical study of Hills mechanism and binary disruption scenario for S-stars and hypervelocity stars. Mentions Koposov et al. 2020 discovery as motivation but paper itself is modeling/analysis without presenting new observational object-level data.
